food_name: Kyat Gway Toke (Burmese Chicken Noodle Soup)

local_names.romanized_local: ကြက်ဂွေးတောက် (Kyat Gway Toke)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to Burmese kyat gway toke exists. FOODON_00002473 (chicken noodle soup) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Burmese chicken noodle soup. A0CKP (noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese kyat gway toke or equivalent Burmese noodle soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of kyat gway toke is approximately 400 g, comprising noodles, chicken pieces, and broth with garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken noodle soup, home-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 06411) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table analogous noodle soup entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct nutrient entry for Burmese kyat gway toke exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken noodle soup (NDB 06411, FDC ID 172956) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle soup analogues. The dish is a clear chicken broth-based noodle soup with chicken pieces, structurally similar to the USDA reference. Values are adjusted modestly upward for sodium to reflect typical Burmese fish-sauce seasoning. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodle soup and wheat noodle soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Burmese kyat gway toke found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous noodle soup entries: rice noodles in broth GI ~40–50 (University of Sydney GI Database); wheat noodle soup GI ~47 (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2008). A midpoint of 45 is used. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as (45 × 30 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and chicken broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a 400 g serving provides approximately 1680 mg sodium, which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from noodles with low-to-moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume kyat gway toke in controlled portions; the estimated GI of 45 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 13.5 per bowl are manageable within a structured meal plan, but noodle quantity should be monitored to avoid postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content inherent in fish-sauce-seasoned broth and should prefer lean chicken breast over dark meat to minimise saturated fat intake per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kyat Gway Toke (ကြက်ဂွေးတောက်) is a traditional Burmese chicken noodle soup widely consumed in Myanmar and served in Burmese restaurants in Singapore. The dish consists of thin rice or wheat noodles submerged in a clear, aromatic chicken broth seasoned with fish sauce, garlic, ginger, turmeric, and onion, topped with shredded or sliced chicken pieces and garnished with spring onion and coriander. The broth is typically simmered for an extended period to develop a savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile. The texture combines soft noodles with tender chicken in a light but flavourful soup. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (approximately 62 kcal per 100 g), provides a useful source of protein from chicken, and contains moderate carbohydrate from noodles. The primary clinical concern is sodium, which is elevated due to fish sauce and broth concentration; a standard 400 g bowl may deliver approximately 1680 mg sodium. The estimated glycemic index is 45 (low), making it relatively suitable for glycemic management when portion-controlled. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified chicken and fish sauce.

Values are estimated from the closest USDA SR Legacy chicken noodle soup entry (FDC 172956) with cross-reference to FAO/INFOODS SEA analogues. GI is estimated from analogous noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the analogous estimation basis.
